bibliographic_data.title: Mapping auroral activity with Twitter
bibliographic_data.abstract: Twitter is a popular, publicly accessible, social media service that has proven useful in mapping large-scale events in real time. In this study, for the first time, the use of Twitter as a measure of auroral activity is investigated. Peaks in the number of aurora-related tweets are found to frequently coincide with geomagnetic disturbances (detection rate of 91%). Additionally, the number of daily aurora-related tweets is found to strongly correlate with several auroral strength proxies (ravg ≈0.7). An examination is made of the bias for location and time of day within Twitter data, and a first-order correction of these effects is presented. Overall, the results suggest that Twitter can provide both specific details about an individual aurora and accurate real-time indication of when, and even from where, an aurora is visible.

core_content.novelty_claim: This study investigates using Twitter as a measure of auroral activity for the first time, showing strong correlation between tweet counts and auroral strength proxies.
core_content.key_methodology: Tweets were collected from September 2012 to April 2013 by the Aurorasaurus citizen science project. The tweets were filtered using aurora-related keywords and compared with several geomagnetic indices as proxies for auroral activity.
outcomes: Peaks in the number of aurora-related tweets coincide with geomagnetic disturbances (91% detection rate)
Daily tweet occurrences strongly correlate with auroral strength proxies (ravg ≈0.7)
Bias for location and time of day within Twitter data was examined and corrected
core_content.full_text_markdown: This study investigates the use of Twitter as a measure of auroral activity, finding that peaks in the number of aurora-related tweets frequently coincide with geomagnetic disturbances (91% detection rate). The number of daily aurora-related tweets also strongly correlates with several auroral strength proxies (ravg ≈0.7). The study examines biases for location and time of day within Twitter data and presents a first-order correction for these effects. Overall, the results suggest that Twitter can provide both specific details about an individual aurora and accurate real-time indication of when, and even from where, an aurora is visible.
